proves impossible, the generated samples are indistinguishable from the real examples, and we have succeeded. If it is possible, the discriminator provides a signal that can be used to improve the generation process.

Figure 15.1 illustrates this scheme. We start with a training set $\lbrace x_{i} \rbrace$ of real 1D examples. A different batch of ten of these examples $\lbrace x_{i} \rbrace_{i=1}^{10}$ is shown in each panel (cyan arrows). To create a batch of samples $\lb
where latent variables $\lbrace z_{j} \rbrace$ are drawn from a standard normal distribution, and the parameter $\theta$ translates the generated samples along the x-axis (figure 15.1).

We alternate between updating the discriminator and the generator. Figures 15.1b–c show two iterations of this process. It gradually becomes harder to classify the data, so the impetus to change $\theta$ becomes weaker (i.e., the sigmoid becomes flatter). At t
